judges.name: billing_security
findings: CRITICAL: claimPayment settles the FULL pre-authorized amount on-chain — actual_amount is logged but never used for settlement. Users are systematically overcharged. The 1.5x preauth ceiling (line 568-569) mitigates but doesn't fix: a request using 10% of max_tokens still pays 1.5x the worst-case estimate.

CRITICAL: No refund/release mechanism when vLLM fails AFTER authorize_spend succeeds (lines 678-688, 727-728). Funds are locked until SpendAuth expiry. Documented but still causes real money loss — operator should attempt a releaseSpend or the contract needs one.

MEDIUM: Nonce store persistence is optional (nonce_store_path defaults to None). Operator restart clears all nonces, allowing replay of any unexpired SpendAuth signatures. This should default to a path, not be opt-in.

MEDIUM: service_id is Option<u64> (config.rs:59) — when None, the service_id check (server.rs:461-473) is skipped entirely. A SpendAuth signed for a different service would pass validation and waste gas on an authorizeSpend the contract rejects.
LOW: CorsLayer::permissive() on a payment API (line 182) allows any origin to submit billing requests. Should restrict to known frontend origins or require API keys.

LOW: spend_auth.amount re-parsed with unwrap_or(0) at line 565 after already being validated at line 393. If somehow different (impossible in current flow), the 0 value bypasses the preauth ceiling check silently.

INFO: Billing failures in settle_billing are logged at error level but the response to the user has already been sent (fire-and-forget pattern at lines 698-708, 844-855). The operator absorbs the loss silently — no alerting mechanism beyond logs.

judges.name: reliability
findings: MEDIUM: Watchdog respawn loop (lib.rs:261-285) has no max retry count or circuit breaker. If vLLM can never start (GPU failure, corrupt model, OOM), this loops forever with backoff capped at 120s, consuming resources indefinitely. A max_respawn_attempts config with an alert/shutdown after exhaustion would prevent a zombie operator.

MEDIUM: ctrl_c handler in watchdog (lib.rs:242) conflicts with BlueprintRunner's own shutdown handling. Both install signal handlers — race condition determines which fires first. If BlueprintRunner's handler wins, the watchdog never cleans up; if the watchdog wins, BlueprintRunner may not drain gracefully.

LOW: line_buf overflow handling (server.rs:775-782) clears the entire buffer, discarding any partial SSE line. This can silently corrupt a streaming response mid-flight. Should find the last newline and keep the tail, or abort the stream cleanly.

LOW: settle_billing logs actual_cost vs preauth but the contract always settles full preauth (documented at server.rs:322-325). The 1.5x ceiling (server.rs:568) mitigates but doesn't eliminate user overcharging — e.g., a request that uses 100 tokens but preauthed for 1.5x of max_tokens=4096 still overpays significantly.

INFO: Provider is rebuilt on every billing RPC call (billing.rs:156-158, 217-219). Under high throughput this creates unnecessary TCP connections. A persistent provider would reduce latency and connection churn.
PASS: stdout/stderr drained via tokio BufReader spawned tasks (vllm.rs:73-90) — no pipe deadlock risk.
PASS: Crash recovery implemented via health-check watchdog with exponential backoff (lib.rs:234-287).
PASS: No .expect()/.unwrap() in fallible production paths. The two .expect() calls (server.rs:891,936) are on Response::builder() with hardcoded valid inputs — infallible in practice.
PASS: Request timeouts implemented at three layers: global TimeoutLayer (server.rs:179), per-chunk idle timeout for SSE (server.rs:744-762), and reqwest 300s client timeout (vllm.rs:23,93).

PASS: Semaphore concurrency limiting correct — try_acquire_owned returns 429 (server.rs:366), permit held through streaming via background task (server.rs:881), per-account limiting via RAII guard (server.rs:583-603).

judges.name: code_quality
findings: thiserror is in Cargo.toml but never used — no custom error types defined. Either remove the dep or define a proper error enum instead of anyhow::Result + RunnerError::Other everywhere.
SSE line-parsing logic is duplicated between server.rs (lines 768-831) and test_sse_chunk_boundary_splits (lines 266-299). Extract into a testable function to avoid drift.

OnceLock<VllmEndpoint> (lib.rs:54) can only be set once. After a vLLM respawn, register_vllm_endpoint silently no-ops (line 77: `let _ = VLLM_ENDPOINT.set(endpoint)`). Works by accident because host:port don't change, but if config were updated on respawn, the stale endpoint would be used. Should be an ArcSwap or RwLock.
CorsLayer::permissive() (server.rs:182) in production allows any origin. Should be configurable or restricted to known frontends.

Watchdog loop (lib.rs:253) checks `vllm_handle.is_healthy()` using the original Arc<VllmProcess> after shutdown+respawn. Health check works via HTTP to the same host:port, but the process was killed — `_live_handle` is the live process, yet it's never used for health checks. If the new process binds a different port, health checks would target the wrong endpoint.

settle_billing (server.rs:327-355) logs charge_amount but the contract call (billing.claim_payment) ignores it — claimPayment always settles full pre-auth. The comment documents this, but the code calculates actual_cost and charge_amount as if partial settlement works, which is misleading. Either remove the dead calculation or gate it behind a future flag.

No tests for the on-chain job handler (run_inference). The TangleArg extraction makes it hard to call directly, but the function could be refactored to accept the raw InferenceRequest, making it testable without the extractor.
uuid and bytes crates in Cargo.toml appear unused in any source file — dead dependencies.

Per-account concurrency guard uses std::sync::RwLock (server.rs:135) which is fine for brief holds, but the lock is acquired inside an async handler. If the map grows very large under load, contention could block the runtime thread. tokio::sync::RwLock or dashmap would be safer at scale.
No request-level tracing (request ID / trace ID). The metrics guard tracks counts and durations, but individual request correlation across log lines (billing, vLLM proxy, settlement) is absent.
